name: Shale Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Shale. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Shale surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Shale mineral absorption characteristics

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective Shale cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous Shale surface processing

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective Shale material removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient Shale ablation with minimal heat diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Shale surface treatment

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete Shale surface cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform Shale surface cleaning

power.description: Typical range for industrial ns-pulsed fiber lasers at 1064 nm cleaning soft rocks like shale; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and avoids thermal cracking due to shale's low thermal conductivity (~1-2 W/m·K). Max safe before damage ~500 W to prevent substrate ablation. Source: Manufacturer specs (e.g., IPG Photonics YLR series) and studies on laser cleaning of stone (Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on shale; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal fluence delivery without overheating. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., Laserax systems) and case studies on rock surface preparation.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of shale to achieve ablation with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but less common industrially due to cost. Avoid fs for shale to prevent micro-cracking from shock waves. Recommended 30 ns for low reflectivity materials. Source: Laser physics research (Applied Surface Science, 2020) on pulsed laser ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for efficient cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation in low-conductivity shale, leading to thermal stress. Recommended 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: low freq for heavy contamination to allow cooling. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (Journal of Laser Applications, 2019).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging shale (ablation threshold ~2-4 J/cm² for siliceous rocks); below 0.5 J/cm² ineffective for heavy soiling. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning, considering shale's moderate reflectivity (~20-30% at 1064 nm) and melting point (~1200°C). Source: Ablation threshold studies (International Journal of Rock Mechanics, 2017) on laser-rock interactions.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine detail on shale surfaces, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area prep. Recommended 200 μm for uniform energy distribution on irregular shale textures. Source: Equipment specifications (e.g., Coherent Armoni series) and process optimization for stone cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on shale to ensure complete removal without excessive energy input. Recommended 2 passes for industrial surface preparation, allowing incremental cleaning to minimize damage risk. Source: Industry practice and case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of geological samples in Applied Optics, 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for detailed shale cleaning to avoid gaps in irregular surfaces. Recommended 60% for optimal uniformity without overexposure. Source: Process optimization studies (Lasers in Engineering, 2016) on scan strategies for rock substrates.
